mentions.note: Own it, don't trade it. China ban is worst-case already priced in; new chips ahead of current ones. Buy at $160.
Don't take the selling personally; Cramer loves the stock and says own it, don't trade it. Selling driven by speculators raising cash for SpaceX/Anthropic/OpenAI, not fundamental weakness.
Major source of liquidity for IPO speculators. Selling is not a negative on the stock itself. Own it, don't trade it. Much IPO capital will eventually buy Nvidia equipment.
Like Apple, selling driven by speculators cashing in for IPO participation, not fundamental weakness. Cramer: own it, don't trade it. Will benefit from SpaceX/Anthropic/OpenAI capex.

Same forced selling as Apple for SpaceX/Anthropic/OpenAI fundraising. Cramer loves Nvidia; own it, don't trade it. Will be a primary beneficiary of mega-IPO proceeds (used for AI chip purchases). Ignore near-term selling pressure.

Massive selling source for SpaceX IPO funding. Excellent company fundamentally; selling is forced liquidation. Own it, don't trade it. Will likely benefit when IPO capital redeploys into AI infrastructure.
Only mega-cap tech with pristine buyback and balance sheet; will benefit from all data center IPOs; being slaughtered unfairly and will snap back first out of the tech mass.

Market linchpin; power behind generative AI, agents, training. Sovereign AI adoption (South Korea, Singapore, India, Japan, etc.) now 14% of revenue and growing, reducing hyperscaler-defection risk. Google paying $920M/month to SpaceX for Nvidia GPUs proves customer preference for Nvidia over in-house chips. Own it long-term, don't trade. Near-term IPO wave will pressure valuations, but Nvidia's strategic moat is intact.
Stalled at $82 — entry point. Banks win structurally when short rates fall and long rates hold.
Post-Discover merger; default rate far lower than expected. Add more if you own it.
Up 3% to all-time high the same day travel/leisure sold off — consumer spending thesis intact.
Crushed today which Cramer says makes no sense. Oversold vs. AXP strength. No explicit buy call but tone is bullish.
Trust position, down 1% today. Needs bigger rate cuts to move meaningfully. Will go higher but slowly — don't sell, don't add yet.
Yielding 3%, a magical level. Fed rate cuts now unlikely, so stock depressed. Long-term holder since $30 should buy more. Stock at 20x spring-selling-season earnings.
Hit all-time high but Cramer cautious on cyclicals broadly unless tied to data center or aerospace.
Reports earnings tomorrow. 25 bps cut won't move the needle for this cycle. Avoid ahead of print.
7.8% dividend yield is a red flag — market is saying don't trust the dividend. May not be sustainable despite management guidance.
CEO Sanjay Mehrotra is fabulous but prudent — may not deliver the hyperbolic language Wall Street wants after stock's +80% YTD run. Wait for post-earnings clarity.
Buy every dip. Wind headwinds are noise — this is fundamentally a natural gas story. Anti-wind policy created the entry point.
Down 27% YTD at 21x forward earnings. Buy case better than sell case. OK to buy if you don't own any; will pound the table lower.
Elliott Management took $2B+ stake with rare supportive press release. $5B buyback, 97% gross retention, 75M users. AI is a tailwind not headwind. Management endorsed.
Investor Day 3-yr targets: mid-single-digit revenue CAGR, +100-150 bps op margin. Trading ~20x vs S&P at 25x with ~20% EPS growth. Still bullish, leaving competition behind.
Emphatic. Doing incredibly well. International growth just scratching the surface. Compared to Celsius pre-Pepsi distribution deal trajectory.
Stopped liking after they sued Costco. American consumer wants bargains. Down 57% but still no interest. Prefers Gap/Athleta instead.

Down 45% YTD, 65% in 12 months. Q missed, guidance dismal: North America same-store sales down 5–6%, expected low double-digit declines next Q. Margin pressure, tariffs, product refresh needed. Trades 10x forward earnings (vs. 30x historical). Bubble burst from pandemic demand pull-forward, explosive competition (Alo, Hoka, Nike, Adidas), style shift to baggier denim, lost pricing power. May be worth a look at much lower levels, not here.
Preferred over Lululemon. Athleta turnaround thesis.
Athletic division net sales down 12%, same-store sales down 11%. Rebranding effort underway but early. Long turnaround project; trades 9x earnings. Athleisure weakness is secular, not cyclical.
Up 8% on big beat; +30% YTD. Non-res business grew 15% in the quarter on data center, reshoring, biotech/pharma construction. Stop overthinking it — just buy Ferguson.
Top-3 S&P 500 performer YTD; at 20x earnings that's a lot for a storage company. Don't chase this high — wait for a pullback.
Just another outsourced company. No strong dislike but no conviction either.
Last quarter was good; stock drop on earnings was unwarranted. Likes it.
Preferred over Eagle Materials (EXP) for building materials exposure.
Had a nice run but Cramer prefers Nucor (NUE) — better balance sheet, expansion plan, less leveraged to autos.
Preferred steel play over Cleveland-Cliffs. Strong balance sheet, expansion plan, less auto exposure.
Christian Klein delivered a great quarter. Would buy SAP right here.
Stuck in split-off limbo until November business separation. Fundamentals good, CEO Lori Koch terrific. Hold — do not sell.
IPO at $33, parabolic to $142 intraday, now ~$54. Price/revenue multiple of 178x. Plain nuts. Anyone who paid the high had no idea what they were buying.
IPO at $37, ran to $118, now ~$54. Unsustainable parabolic IPO move.
IPO at $31, ran to $298, now ~$131. Still way too high. Stablecoin company; speculation not fundamentals.

IPO priced at $135 but at risk of catastrophic blowout if syndicate desks lose control; retail mania and unguided market orders could spike it to 4–5 trillion valuation unsustainably. Monitor opening behavior closely; do not chase if it gaps violently higher.
IPO priced at $1.75T; syndicate desk control is critical. Uncontrolled retail demand and index inclusion could spark unsustainable spike to $4-5T valuation, then correction—avoid chasing.

IPO reportedly 4x oversubscribed but Cramer wants to see 10x to be confident. Key risk: speculator cohort will flip shares on day one, creating oversupply and poor opening performance (like Figma, Cerebrus). Best IPO outcome: retail + early-stage institutions (non-sellers); worst: dominated by flippers. Warns allocation holders: be cautious if you get full ask or over-allocation.
Risk of market-order-driven opening spike (valuation $5T) followed by sharp reversal like Cerebrus deal; retail inflows may be too hot; no near-term profitability; IPO execution risk high.

Insider buying by Cash Patel, Congressman Moore, and a director ($1.5M) is constructive; company appears undervalued. However, Cramer refuses to speculate on Berkshire Hathaway M&A and advises holding based on fundamentals, not acquisition rumor.

Post-spin-off Honeywell Technologies is a pure-play automation company with secular growth in mission-critical verticals (data centers, hospitals, LNG). 2/3 of global LNG capacity, $1B+ backlog, and physical AI optionality. Long-cycle revenue, resilient to macro. Cramer's child trust holds a large position; seeks to increase.

Pure-play automation company post-aerospace spin-off; mission-critical markets (data centers, hotels, hospitals, LNG). 2/3 of global LNG supply, 3-year backlog, secular growth. Cramer's trust owns large position, wants bigger. Not expensive relative to growth path.

Gridscale battery storage with $5.5B signed contracts and strong backlog, but thin/inconsistent margins. Up 62% YTD. Cramer insists profitability must arrive before stock appreciates further; not a buy until margins inflect.
Grid-scale battery storage and software play with strong $5.5B backlog, but inconsistent thin margins. Must achieve profitability before higher stock prices justified; caller up 62% since April.
Industrial IoT pure-play with solid fundamentals. Cramer endorses a full position, recommending caller add the second half of his position. Terrific long-term holding.
Industrial internet exposure; strong fundamentals. Caller should add to half position and complete full holding.

Oil plays are paradoxical to time on Trump rhetoric: when he says talks are going well, oil weakens (sell); when he warns of escalation, oil rallies (buy). Today's Iran strike cancellation drove oil lower. Focus on fundamentals and domestic supply insulation rather than geopolitical headlines.
Oil price volatile with Iran headlines. Trump's contradictory statements (seize Kharg Island vs. cancel strikes) create reverse-signal dynamics—buy on bad news, sell on good news rhetoric.
Preferred large-cap oil play over BP.

Semiconductor equipment beneficiary of chip storage/memory shortage; CEO Gary Dickerson well-positioned in the food chain. New intraday high despite tough market backdrop.

Data center optical fiber and infrastructure play. Cramer prefers over AAOI despite poor chart. Better fundamentals; willing to hold through technical weakness.
Shedding slow-growth divisions; refocusing on fast-growing drugs; AAA balance sheet; emerging leadership stock in healthcare sector.

Core defensive holding in the charitable trust providing cushion during today's data-center meltdown. Strong new drug pipeline, triple-A balance sheet better than U.S. Preserved sanity during tsunami sell-off.
Pet food, coffee, peanut butter portfolio has scarcity value and decent balance sheet; food stocks offer alternative leadership in uncertain times.
Retail beneficiary of strong housing market; good housing sales support home-goods retailers; two thumbs up.
Cramer wants to buy Netflix. Warner Bros. studio contemplation was prudent optionality review; down 13% is good entry; will outperform.
Earnings missed badly; Cramer's trust lost heavily; compared to a weekend of hell in 1982; semiconductor oversupply headwind.
Leading workforce development initiative (America's Workforce Academy) addressing skilled labor shortage; bullish on company vision and data center investment thesis.
Potential equity issuance to fund data-center capex could dilute shareholder base alongside Google and Amazon offerings, draining market liquidity pre-SpaceX IPO.
Considering colossal fund raise for AI ambitions (similar to Alphabet's multi-billion move). Supply of new stock flooding market is a headwind; investors must sell other holdings to fund purchases.

Bot traffic exceeds human traffic; AI agents driving exponential internet growth; Cloudflare powers this infrastructure; layoffs were prudent restructuring; platform positioning strong for next 27 years of internet business model.

Secular growth utility (not just dividend staid); $67B capital plan doubled; Meta data center partnership; fair-share-plus model ensures customer balance; nuclear expansion potential; been recommending since 2012.
Media sector decimated by AI; Cramer cannot get behind legacy publisher narrative regardless of Wall Street skepticism.
Injectables play; Cramer approves for spec portfolios; one spec stock is all investors need to scratch that itch responsibly.
Military drone autonomous systems meme stock; too dangerous in this horrible market; meme stocks can rip lungs out; Cramer won't allow it.
Was favorite stock until market turned ugly; market facts changed; let it come down before reconsidering; no longer right coloration for speculation.
Cramer prefers other alternatives; no strong conviction on Wix itself relative to market conditions.
Low multiple appeal but Cramer prefers Viking Cruises (VKG) long-term; Viking offers better 5-year outlook; Carnival reasonable hold but not best-in-class.
Preferred cruise line over Carnival for next 5 years; better fundamental positioning; Cramer recommends sailing with Viking.
Developing Trainium and Trainium alternatives to reduce Nvidia chip reliance. If equity offering follows Google's, will dilute supply further just before SpaceX IPO.
May do a fundraiser per Cramer's speculation. Supply could hit stock if they tap capital markets.
Equity dilution risk similar to Alphabet and Amazon if capital needs force secondary offerings before SpaceX IPO closes.
May do a fundraiser per Cramer's speculation. Unknown if they'll need to raise capital for AI/infrastructure.

Botched IPO pricing; opened at $370 vs. expected $170–180 range and has collapsed since. Caller Angel underwater on recent purchase. Classic example of overallocation and mispricing Cramer fears for SpaceX this week.
Expensive software-as-a-service company pivoting rapidly; mixed fundamentals. Better than most enterprise software peers, but not a buy at current levels in today's cautious environment.

German distributed power-generation play for data centers; 7x backlog expansion and 60% order growth offset margin erosion from production ramp and raw-material costs. Enterprise EV/EBITDA multiple of 46 not cheap. Prefer entry around 29, but small position justified here.

CEO Jim Conroy's merchandising, marketing, and in-store improvements driving 17% comp growth, 20% revenue growth, 27% earnings growth. Best performer in off-price space; 47% gain since he arrived. Off-price retail is one of few retail sectors working. Worth owning.
Trading at 47x earnings; buy some here, hope for pullback to 45x. Ladder in rather than chasing. Worst case opens up to $1025 and you exit.
Cosmetics group broadly challenged; can't buck sectoral weakness. Good company but sector too troubled to touch.
Bitcoin/crypto holding company. If you want Bitcoin, buy Bitcoin. Mike Novogratz is solid and company has positive traits, but avoid the equity.
Recommended over Galaxy Digital for quantum exposure; cleaner play than crypto-linked companies.
Fine oil company but Chevron preferred for large-cap exposure; EQT preferred for natural gas.
Stock broke down for no clear reason. Buy a little here, then add if it declines further. Chart became ugly but bounce opportunity exists.
Numbers are bad. Possibly end of COVID-era urban-to-rural trade. Can't recommend until clarity returns; CEO Hal Lawton is solid but fundamentals unexplained.

Down 22% YTD with 7.2% yield signaling dividend coverage concerns. Food industry needs consolidation or faces low-dollar stocks unable to maintain dividends; no growth, unhealthy, hit by GOP cuts, no pricing power. Reports Monday.
Rebounding recently but faces headwind from high gasoline ($110 fill-up noted). Discretionary travel vulnerable if fuel stays elevated.
Last report saw stock jump on okay quarter then get smashed. Rally in offbeat names like this may reverse when it reports Tuesday; owns Twinkies, coffee, dog food, Uncrustables.
Dominates small-town America with breakfast pizza; reports after close. Results TBD.
Up 32% YTD but down from $71 to $33 in ~12 months. Stock closed at $33+; Cramer would like to buy but wants to see earnings first—consumer weakness in pet/discretionary spending is concerning.
Pet spending never supposed to suffer, but missed quarter badly. Chewy may miss too given consumer weakness.
Part of shrinking SaaS cohort with cheaper competitors in design software space. Even after thrashing, not low enough to own; needs further decline.
Home building punches above its weight at 10% of economy. Needs lower interest rates to drive more home sales; rising rate expectations hurt outlook.

High school caller asks about entry amid U.S. conflict uncertainty. Cramer advises dollar-cost averaging: buy 2 shares now, then 3 more if it drops below $500 (stock hit ~$700 recently). Avoid chasing; be disciplined.

$80B company providing opto-electronic components critical for AI buildout, up 100%+ YTD. Going parabolic. Cramer advises selling half here (even if down 25%) and waiting to buy back the inevitable crash. Parabolic moves always reverse hard.

Just transferred to NYSE (largest in 234 years). Targeting $80B revenue by 2030, on track. Investing $50B in U.S. manufacturing and R&D. Oncology booming (Tagriso combos, new breast/liver cancer treatments). GLP-1 weight management pipeline competitive with Eli Lilly—Phase 3 candidate, monthly injectables, muscle-loss mitigation. AI boosting drug discovery productivity and trial success prediction. CEO confident on ambitious targets.

Revenue down 3%, Nike Direct down 7%, Digital down 9%, Converse down 35%. Gross margin fell 130 bp to 40.2%. Lost pricing power, stale products, competition rife. CEO Elliott Hill trying turnaround but taking longer than hoped. New CEO gets a chance but burden of proof is on him. Trades 24x earnings, higher than peers due to better relative position but still risky.

Beef cost inflation is real pressure, but 2.6% yield, great reputation, 21x earnings with room to 18–19x. DCA strategy: buy at $280, $279, $270, $265, and 50 shares at $260 to build better cost basis.
Aerospace/utility/semiconductor testing equipment spinoff from Florida; strong recent quarter and buyback underway. But stock is parabolic—don't chase parabolas in this market.
10-year breakout on electric drivetrain pivot and gas turbine opportunity—been an absolute winner. But parabolic; could drop from $72 to $60 in a heartbeat. Wait for the inevitable correction.
Cramer likes it—very good unique property—but at 29x earnings, too expensive to buy aggressively. Buy slowly over time, not all at once.
Business development company trading 20% discount to NAV with 11% yield. Cramer refuses BDC recommendations—doesn't know what they own and won't start now. Too risky.
CEO appears checked out per caller; hasn't raised dividend in 12 years. Commercial real estate in weak cities. Not been a good stock; Cramer has not recommended it.

Mega IPO driving massive cash raises by other tech firms. Won't fast-track into S&P 500 (stays under 1-year rule + profitability requirement). May not achieve profitability soon given heavy Starship/Starlink/AI infrastructure investments. Supply deluge from IPO is crushing broader market.

Huge winner for the charitable trust; sold some at $310 but let it run to $400 without trimming. Lost discipline. Down 12%+ today. Should have exited more aggressively. Lesson: trim parabolic winners even when hypnotized by gains—take 'little schnitle' off table.

Core defensive holding in the charitable trust providing cushion during today's tech meltdown. Acts as a 'permit' enabling safe ownership of riskier growth plays. Doesn't boom but doesn't crash either.

ODFL.company: Old Dominion Freight Line / FedEx Freight (FXFV spinoff)

mentions.note: New spinoff of FedEx Freight held in charitable trust. Less-than-truckload operator with potential to become one of world's best. Diversification play—doesn't need to be a data-center money maker to work.
